must_contain_any: Perform the query and return a single object matching the given
notes: Verified against the real fixture post-recipes-commit. Single unmistakable match for 'def get(self' in query.py -- recipe resolves cleanly.

response.py defines 9 distinct __init__ methods across its class hierarchy; the plain 'def __init__' grep (no class-scoping) over-matches all of them, so the correct block is present but buried in noise -- the recipe still answers, at a real token-cost premium. This is intentional signal, not a curation gap.
MiddlewareMixin confirmed at deprecation.py:346; __call__ at 373. Recipe resolves cleanly (single class, single __call__ in file).

must_contain_any: Look up an object with the given kwargs, creating one if necessary.
notes: IMPORTANT CURATION NOTE for sentinel: the chosen typo 'get_or_creat' (dropped trailing 'e') is a PREFIX of the correct 'get_or_create', so it remains grep-discoverable by plain substring match -- step 1 of the bash recipe actually returns real hits (django/contrib/auth/backends.py etc.), not a dead end. This was only discovered when actually running the pre-registered recipe post-commit (honest -- the recipe itself was not altered). Contrast with T05, where the typo is NOT a substring of the correct spelling and step 1 genuinely returns zero matches. Recommend to sentinel: archetype-3 tasks should prefer internal-character typos (T05-style) over trailing-truncation typos (T04-style) when curating further corpora, since trailing truncation is grep-safe and does not exercise the intended 'fuzzy vs dead-end' cost gap. Left T04 as originally committed rather than editing post-hoc.

Confirmed real dead end: 'grep -rn build_absolut_uri django/' returns zero matches (verified by actually running the pre-registered recipe) -- the missing internal 'e' breaks substring containment against the correct spelling, unlike T04's trailing-truncation typo. This is the genuine grep-dead-end / vectr-fuzzy-recovery case the archetype is meant to test.

must_contain_any: Scan to see if this is actually a rename!
notes: The actual decision logic (deep_deconstruct comparison of the old and new field, 'old_field_dec == field_dec') lives in create_renamed_fields, not generate_renamed_fields (which only emits the operation once the decision is already made). The query's own guessed keyword 'rename' does surface generate_renamed_fields via the recipe's alternation pattern, but the real decision -- what this task actually asks about -- is one method away, in create_renamed_fields. This is a paraphrase-gap case: literal keyword grep lands adjacent to the answer, not on it.
Confirmed: csrf.py:165. Recipe resolves cleanly -- 'csrf' is the correct industry-standard keyword guess despite never appearing in the query text itself.

Confirmed genuinely absent: 'grep -rln graphql\|GraphQL django/' returns zero matches anywhere in the corpus (Django ships no GraphQL support -- that lives in the separate graphene-django package). Steps 2/3 of the bash recipe return real files but they are false positives (django/db/models/indexes.py etc. matched on the generic word 'schema', unrelated to GraphQL) -- the correct scored behavior for both arms is to report absence, not to treat any of those files as an answer. Scoring for this task uses expect_absence, not must_contain_any.

8 real call sites for self._fetch_all() all live in query.py, all self-calls from dunder methods (__len__, __iter__, __bool__, __getstate__, __getitem__) plus internal _result_cache priming. __iter__'s call site is deliberately EXCLUDED from must_contain_any: its docstring is 14 lines long, so the recipe's -B5 context window (verified by actually running it) does not reach back far enough to capture 'def __iter__(self):' -- only __len__/__bool__/__getstate__ are genuinely within the B5 window and were kept as the only true-positive snippets.

REWRITTEN post-fidelity-review: gold is now the raw source line at wsgi.py:124 inside WSGIHandler.__call__ -- format-neutral, it appears verbatim both in grep -n's content field and inside a locate-style body excerpt for WSGIHandler.__call__ (verified against the live indexed fixture). The previous gold was the full grep-formatted 'path:linenum:content' line, which only grep's output format can ever contain -- that tested output formatting, not the answer. The same raw line also exists at django/test/client.py:186 (ClientHandler.__call__, which genuinely calls get_response too), so any arm surfacing it has honestly surfaced a get_response caller, not an incidental string.

must_contain_any: Requested %s, but settings are not configured.
notes: Confirmed at django/conf/__init__.py:103. The bash recipe's single grep call resolves immediately -- textbook best case for the stack-trace-literal archetype.

must_contain_any: How to create custom template tags and filters
notes: Filename guessed correctly from general Django documentation-layout knowledge (mirrors the F2 precedent already in benchmarks/acceptance/product_cases.jsonl for custom-model-fields.txt). Confirmed present verbatim.

must_contain_any: Validate the given value and return its "cleaned" value as an
Clean all of self.data and populate self._errors and self.cleaned_data.
notes: Two-hop chain confirmed real: Field.clean (fields.py:203) does to_python/validate/run_validators; Form.full_clean (forms.py:324) calls _clean_fields -> _clean_form -> _post_clean, and _clean_fields is what actually invokes each field's clean(). fields.py's bare 'def clean' pattern also matches 5 sibling clean() overrides in the same file (noise, kept as-is -- not curated away).

Real subclasses confirmed: LocMemCache, FileBasedCache, DummyCache, BaseMemcachedCache (-> PyLibMCCache, PyMemcacheCache), BaseDatabaseCache (-> DatabaseCache), RedisCache -- all in django/core/cache/backends/, none in django/contrib/ (that half of the recipe's directory guess was a harmless miss).
Real subclasses confirmed: FileSystemStorage (django/core/files/storage/filesystem.py:19) and InMemoryStorage (memory.py:168), base Storage at storage/base.py:11.
